content.problemStatement:
Write a program that takes the radius of a circle as input and prints its area.

The formula of the area of a circle is pi∗radius∗radiuspi * radius * radiuspi∗radius∗radius.

Take the value of pi to be 3.143.143.14
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single floating‑point number R (0 < R ≤ 1000) representing the radius of a circle.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the area of the circle on its own line, formatted to exactly five digits after the decimal point.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First radius 5 gives 78.53500, second radius 10 gives 3.1414 × 10 × 10 = 314.14000.
Same as sample 2 with an extra test case of radius 10.

solution.approach: The area of a circle is given by π·r·r. The problem fixes π = 3.1414. For each test case we read the radius, compute area = 3.1414 * r * r using double precision, and print the result with exactly five digits after the decimal point. The algorithm is O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time computation per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few scalar variables are used.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sbAll=new StringBuilder();String line;while((line=br.readLine())!=null){sbAll.append(line).append('\n');}String raw=sbAll.toString().replace("\\n"," ");StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(raw);if(!st.hasMoreTokens())return;int T=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());final double PI=3.1414;StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();for(int i=0;i<T;i++){if(!st.hasMoreTokens())break;double r=Double.parseDouble(st.nextToken());double area=PI*r*r;out.append(String.format(java.util.Locale.US,"%.5f",area));if(i+1<T)out.append("\\n");}System.out.print(out.toString());}}
